narration_segments.text: The loop starts before a person knows it has started. That is its nature. That is its power. That is the reason it persists. A thought arrives. Reasonable. Ordinary. "Did I handle that conversation well." The mind considers the question. It replays the conversation. It edits the replay — not accurately, but persuasively. It adds emphasis where none existed. It sharpens a tone that was actually neutral. It manufactures a subtext that the other person never intended. It runs the conversation through a filter of self-doubt that distorts every word. And then the loop begins.

Michi is twenty-nine. She works as an editor at a publishing house in Tokyo. She is extraordinarily good at finding errors. She catches misplaced commas, inconsistent character names, factual contradictions buried on page three hundred that reference a detail from page forty. She holds the full architecture of a manuscript in her memory and scans it the way a security system scans a building — constantly, automatically, without rest. This skill makes her excellent at her job. It makes her unbearable to herself. Because the same mechanism that catches errors in manuscripts catches errors in conversation. In relationships. In decisions made three years ago that cannot be unmade. Her pattern recognition does not have an off switch. It does not distinguish between a manuscript and a text message. Between a client's novel and her own life. A typical evening: Michi finishes dinner. She sits on the floor of her apartment. She picks up her phone to send a message to a friend — a simple message, a plan for Saturday. She types three words. Deletes them. Types five words. Reads them. Reads them again. Considers how the friend might interpret the second word. Changes it. Reads the new version.

This chapter is a practice. The listener does it now. Not later. Not after reading the next chapter. Now. Sit down. Any surface. Let the back be straight but not stiff. Junko says: "Sit like you are waiting for a friend at a quiet cafe. Not slumped. Not at attention. Not performing. Just present. Ready. At ease." Place both hands flat on the thighs. Palms down. Feel the weight of the hands. Feel the warmth of the palms against the fabric or the skin. Feel the shape of the thigh beneath the hand — the curve of the muscle, the firmness of the bone underneath. This is the anchor. Whenever the mind pulls away — and it will pull, immediately and repeatedly — the hands bring it back. The hands are the landing strip. Close the eyes or lower them. First: arrive. Arriving means noticing the body in the chair.

The listener re-enters the day. The practice is portable. It requires no cushion, no silence, no special conditions, no specific time. It requires hands, a surface, and the willingness to land. Junko does not promise enlightenment. She does not promise a quiet mind. She does not promise the end of overthinking. She promises a shorter loop. She promises that the distance between the second pass and the catch gets smaller with practice. She promises that the body — the hands, the feet, the belly, the breath — becomes a reliable ally in a process the mind cannot solve alone.
